original_abstract:
Russia’s invasion of Ukraine catalyzed a level of Western democratic unity not witnessed since the first Gulf War. Yet, Western anticipations of a synchronized global condemnation of Moscow were quickly tempered by the complex reactions emerging from the Global South. Rather than aligning with the Western consensus, more than 40 member states consistently abstained or voted against UN General Assembly resolutions condemning Russian aggression. This divergence became particularly stark in April 2022, when, despite mounting evidence of human rights atrocities, 50 nations—spanning Africa, Asia, the Middle East, and Latin America—opposed Russia's expulsion from the Human Rights Council. These states largely maintained that the priority should be an immediate cessation of hostilities, regardless of the conflict’s origins or conduct. 

This paper investigates the drivers behind this perceived indifference, and at times open hostility, toward the Western position. By analyzing the invasion through the specific lens of the Global South, the study examines three core pillars: how these nations perceived the Western-led response to the invasion; the subsequent revival of internal debates regarding the principles of non-alignment; and how the conflict has served as a catalyst for renewed demands to reform the existing multilateral system.
humanized_abstract:
The invasion of Ukraine by Russia brought about for the first time since the Gulf War in 1991 a high degree of unity among Western democracies. But expectations in the West that the rest of the world would be in line in terms of opposition to Russia’s actions were disappointed. More than 40 member states have consistently abstained or voted in opposition to General Assembly resolutions sponsored by Western countries aimed at criticising Russia’s actions in Ukraine. There has been particularly stark opposition this month when 50 countries, from Africa, Asia, the Middle East and Latin America voted against the removal of Russia from the UN Human Rights Council, despite mounting evidence in that respect being uncovered by special investigators. Most of these countries have argued that their main priority is an immediate cessation of fighting in Eastern Ukraine, whatever the circumstances and without regard to Russia’s role in that regard.

Why has the international community made what appears to be a deliberately ‘scarcely-noticeable’ and at the very least impolite response to the West’s stance in this case? This short paper aims to explore possible motives behind this reaction and, by viewing the current event in the context of the Global South, delves into three aspects: the attitudes in the Global South toward the Western ‘official reaction’ to the invasion; the revival of old debates regarding the tenets of non-alignment; and how the war seems to be contributing to the calls to reform the global governance system.
